response:
## Results

We evaluated ten experimental variants of our proposed method on the validation set, each averaged over five random seeds. Table 1 summarizes the mean validation accuracy and standard deviation for each configuration. Variant_03, which omits the proposed component and serves as our baseline, achieved a validation accuracy of 67.8% ± 0.6. Among the nine variants incorporating the proposed component (variants_01, 02, 04, 05, 06, 07, 08, 09, and 10), only variant_08 surpassed the baseline, attaining an accuracy of 70.4% ± 0.5—a gain of 2.6 absolute points over the baseline. The remaining eight variants underperformed relative to the baseline, with accuracies ranging from 64.7% to 67.2%.

Specifically, variant_01 achieved 67.2% ± 0.8, variant_02 66.9% ± 0.7, variant_04 65.1% ± 0.9, variant_05 66.3% ± 1.0, variant_06 64.7% ± 0.7, variant_07 65.8% ± 0.8, variant_09 66.5% ± 0.7, and variant_10 65.9% ± 0.8. These results suggest that, in most cases, addition of the proposed component did not yield improvements over the baseline configuration. However, the substantial gain observed in variant_08 demonstrates that, under the specific configuration tested in this variant, the proposed method can provide a meaningful boost in validation accuracy.

Overall, while most instantiations of the proposed method did not outperform the baseline, variant_08 highlights the potential of the approach in certain settings. Further analysis is required to understand the factors contributing to the success of variant_08 and to identify conditions under which the proposed component consistently leads to improvements.
